 230: def get_run(run_id: str, *, path: str | None = None) -> RunStatusResponse | None:
 231:     """Return the run record or None if missing.
 232: 
 233:     Defensive against `OperationalError: no such table` so callers in stub
 234:     mode (e.g. `tests/v6/test_actors.py` direct `.fn()` invocation without
 235:     init_db) get None back, matching the actor's row-missing-stub-noop
 236:     contract.
 237: 
 238:     I-arch-001a Codex iter-2 P2-002: if a known DB exists with the legacy
 239:     schema, the first read-only `get_run` call would 500 on column-missing.
 240:     The defensive catch below detects "no such column" and triggers a
 241:     one-time migration via init_db before retrying.
 242:     """
 243:     conn = _connect(path)
 244:     try:
 245:         try:
 246:             row = conn.execute(
 247:                 "SELECT run_id, template, question, lifecycle_status, pipeline_status, "
 248:                 "queued_at, started_at, finished_at, result_json, error_json, "
 249:                 "query_slug, manifest_run_id, artifact_dir, cost_usd, decision_id "
 250:                 "FROM runs WHERE run_id=?",
 251:                 (run_id,),
 252:             ).fetchone()
 253:         except sqlite3.OperationalError as exc:
 254:             msg = str(exc).lower()
 255:             # Narrow per Codex iter-1 P2-001: only the missing-table stub
 256:             # path returns None; surface other operational errors (schema
 257:             # corruption, migration faults) so they aren't masked as
 258:             # missing-row.
 259:             if "no such table" in msg:
 260:                 return None
 261:             # Codex iter-2 P2-002: legacy schema → migrate then retry once.
 262:             if "no such column" in msg:
 263:                 conn.close()
 264:                 init_db(path)
 265:                 conn = _connect(path)
 266:                 row = conn.execute(
 267:                     "SELECT run_id, template, question, lifecycle_status, pipeline_status, "
 268:                     "queued_at, started_at, finished_at, result_json, error_json, "
 269:                     "query_slug, manifest_run_id, artifact_dir, cost_usd, decision_id "
 270:                     "FROM runs WHERE run_id=?",
 271:                     (run_id,),
 272:                 ).fetchone()
 273:             else:
 274:                 raise
 275:     finally:
 276:         conn.close()
 277:     if row is None:
 278:         return None
 279:     return RunStatusResponse(
 280:         run_id=row["run_id"],
 281:         template=row["template"],
 282:         question=row["question"],
 283:         lifecycle_status=row["lifecycle_status"],
 284:         pipeline_status=row["pipeline_status"],
 285:         queued_at=row["queued_at"],
 286:         started_at=row["started_at"],
 287:         finished_at=row["finished_at"],
 288:         result_json=row["result_json"],
 289:         error_json=row["error_json"],
 290:         query_slug=row["query_slug"],
 291:         manifest_run_id=row["manifest_run_id"],
 292:         artifact_dir=row["artifact_dir"],
 293:         cost_usd=row["cost_usd"],
 294:         decision_id=row["decision_id"],
 295:     )

   1: """Run-status response schema.
   2: 
   3: I-arch-001a (2026-05-12): split lifecycle (operational) from pipeline_status
   4: (pipeline-A manifest verdict). Old `status` retained as computed alias for
   5: tests/v6/ backcompat — populated from lifecycle_status.
   6: """
   7: 
   8: from __future__ import annotations
   9: 
  10: from typing import Literal
  11: 
  12: from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, computed_field
  13: 
  14: LifecycleStatus = Literal[
  15:     "queued",
  16:     "in_progress",
  17:     "completed",
  18:     "cancelled",
  19:     "failed",
  20: ]
  21: 
  22: PipelineStatus = Literal[
  23:     "success",
  24:     "partial_outline_fallback",
  25:     "partial_qwen_advisory",
  26:     "partial_thin_corpus",
  27:     "partial_incomplete_corpus",
  28:     "partial_rule_check_warnings",
  29:     "abort_scope_rejected",
  30:     "abort_corpus_inadequate",
  31:     "abort_corpus_approval_denied",
  32:     "abort_no_verified_sections",
  33:     "abort_no_sources",
  34:     "abort_evaluator_critical",
  35:     "error_unexpected",
  36: ]
  37: 
  38: # Backcompat alias used by older code/tests (tests/v6/ asserts body['status']
  39: # and record.status). Computed from lifecycle_status by the model.
  40: RunStatus = LifecycleStatus
  41: 
  42: 
  43: class RunStatusResponse(BaseModel):
  44:     run_id: str = Field(..., description="Server-assigned UUID hex.")
  45:     lifecycle_status: LifecycleStatus = Field(
  46:         ...,
  47:         description="Operational lifecycle: queued → in_progress → completed|failed|cancelled.",
  48:     )
  49:     pipeline_status: PipelineStatus | None = Field(
  50:         default=None,
  51:         description="Pipeline-A manifest verdict. NULL until run reaches a terminal pipeline state.",
  52:     )
  53:     template: str
  54:     question: str
  55:     queued_at: str = Field(..., description="ISO8601 UTC.")
  56:     started_at: str | None = None
  57:     finished_at: str | None = None
  58:     result_json: str | None = None
  59:     error_json: str | None = None
  60:     # I-arch-001a new optional fields for UUID/slug/artifact_dir mapping.
  61:     query_slug: str | None = None
  62:     manifest_run_id: str | None = None
  63:     artifact_dir: str | None = None
  64:     cost_usd: float | None = None
  65:     decision_id: str | None = None
  66: 
  67:     @computed_field  # serialized in JSON and readable as attr
  68:     @property
  69:     def status(self) -> LifecycleStatus:
  70:         """Deprecated alias for lifecycle_status (kept for tests/v6/ backcompat).
  71: 
  72:         New code should use lifecycle_status. This computed field will be
  73:         removed once tests/v6/ migrate (post-Carney-demo cleanup).
  74:         """
  75:         return self.lifecycle_status

  10: def _no_real_backend_keys(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch):

One concrete blocker is already visible in the new endpoint dependency wiring: the lazy lambda is a different FastAPI dependency key than the production signer override, so the route will resolve `None` even when the app has configured GPG signing. I’m checking the artifact bridge now for comparable execution risks rather than style issues.

outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:7:The ceiling V28 hit is equally real. The four LOSE_BOTH dimensions are not independent failures. Citations, claim frames, structural depth, and narrative depth all failed because the report did not make the pivotal primary publications the spine of the answer. SURPASS-2 was cited through a T4 post-hoc instead of the Frías NEJM primary; SURPASS-4 Del Prato and SURPASS-CVOT Nicholls were in live_corpus but did not reach the final bibliography or prose; mechanism remained review-grade because primary clamp evidence did not become extracted narrative. Once the primary frame was missing, every downstream artifact became compensatory: trial tables were thin, per-trial summaries selected easier trials instead of the important ones, and mechanism prose grew longer without becoming more primary-evidence-dense.
outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:11:The strict_verify gate is both the reason POLARIS is credible and the reason cheap imitation of competitor prose fails. Competitors can quote or synthesize trial numbers from memory or inaccessible primary papers. POLARIS must match claims to fetched direct_quote text. For paywalled NEJM/Lancet primaries with thin fetched text, strict_verify blocks the exact numeric specificity that wins clinical dimensions. The answer is not to discard strict_verify globally. The answer is to make primary publication reachability and quote adequacy a first-class pipeline contract before generation begins.
outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:33:V29 should implement alpha as the first architectural slice: selector-level primary hard reservation plus generator-side named-trial injection from live_corpus. This should recover SURPASS-4/CVOT and prevent post-hoc substitution for SURPASS-2 when a primary exists. Expected outcome: Citations, Claim frames, and Structural depth move from LOSE_BOTH to at least BEAT_ONE, with possible BEAT_BOTH if quote extraction is adequate. Risk: medium. If primary direct_quotes are too thin, the report may cite primaries without extracting the competitor-level ETDs.

outputs\codex_findings\v29_fix_plan_review_pass1\findings.md:19:1. **M-51 stable identity**: do not rely on Python `id(item)` as the durable duplicate check. Use canonical evidence identity: `evidence_id` when present, otherwise a stable fallback key such as normalized `(source_url, title, direct_quote[:N])`. Object identity can miss duplicates if the same row is copied or transformed.
outputs\codex_findings\v29_fix_plan_review_pass1\findings.md:22:4. **M-52 evidence IDs**: preserve live-corpus `evidence_id` if present and not colliding with a different row. Use `ev_from_corpus_{anchor_slug}_{n}` only for missing IDs or real collisions. Add tests for both paths.

outputs\codex_findings\full_audit_pass_5\findings.md:43:PT12 is a real medium and currently gating. The `[2025]` occurrence is in the bibliography title: `[6] Best Guide on RAG Pipeline, Use Cases & Diagrams [2025] ...`, not in report prose as an emitted citation marker. `external_evaluator.py` scans the entire report with `r"\[(\d+)\]"`, so it treats the title year as marker 2025 and aborts because the evidence pool has 19 entries. The fix should restrict PT12 to actual citation-marker contexts or exclude bibliography-title year brackets; as-is, it can block valid reports.

outputs\codex_findings\m42ab_code_audit\findings.md:10:   In `build_trial_summary_and_timeline_from_evidence`, the builder reads `direct_quote` first and attempts `refetch_fn(url, 2000)` when the quote is under 100 chars. outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:7:The ceiling V28 hit is equally real. The four LOSE_BOTH dimensions are not independent failures. Citations, claim frames, structural depth, and narrative depth all failed because the report did not make the pivotal primary publications the spine of the answer. SURPASS-2 was cited through a T4 post-hoc instead of the Frías NEJM primary; SURPASS-4 Del Prato and SURPASS-CVOT Nicholls were in live_corpus but did not reach the final bibliography or prose; mechanism remained review-grade because primary clamp evidence did not become extracted narrative. Once the primary frame was missing, every downstream artifact became compensatory: trial tables were thin, per-trial summaries selected easier trials instead of the important ones, and mechanism prose grew longer without becoming more primary-evidence-dense.
outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:11:The strict_verify gate is both the reason POLARIS is credible and the reason cheap imitation of competitor prose fails. Competitors can quote or synthesize trial numbers from memory or inaccessible primary papers. POLARIS must match claims to fetched direct_quote text. For paywalled NEJM/Lancet primaries with thin fetched text, strict_verify blocks the exact numeric specificity that wins clinical dimensions. The answer is not to discard strict_verify globally. The answer is to make primary publication reachability and quote adequacy a first-class pipeline contract before generation begins.
outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\findings.md:33:V29 should implement alpha as the first architectural slice: selector-level primary hard reservation plus generator-side named-trial injection from live_corpus. This should recover SURPASS-4/CVOT and prevent post-hoc substitution for SURPASS-2 when a primary exists. Expected outcome: Citations, Claim frames, and Structural depth move from LOSE_BOTH to at least BEAT_ONE, with possible BEAT_BOTH if quote extraction is adequate. Risk: medium. If primary direct_quotes are too thin, the report may cite primaries without extracting the competitor-level ETDs.

   1: """Sovereignty guard: legal-cleared spans only (I-f15-006).
   2: 
   3: A bundle MUST refuse to ship verbatim source-text snapshots for sources
   4: whose provenance does NOT explicitly declare `legal_cleared = True`.
   5: The full EvidencePool (including every Source.full_text/snippet) is
   6: serialized into evidence_pool.json by the bundle builder, so EVERY
   7: source in the pool — cited or not — must be cleared.
   8: """
   9: 
  10: from __future__ import annotations
  11: 
  12: from polaris_graph.retrieval2.evidence_pool import EvidencePool
  13: 
  14: LEGAL_CLEARED_KEY = "legal_cleared"
  15: 
  16: 
  17: def assert_all_pool_sources_legal_cleared(pool: EvidencePool) -> None:
  18:     """Raise ValueError if any pool source lacks `provenance.legal_cleared = True`.
  19: 
  20:     Walks EVERY source in the pool (not only cited ones) because the
  21:     full pool serializes into evidence_pool.json — uncited sources still
  22:     get their full_text/snippet redistributed.
  23:     """
  24:     for source in pool.sources:
  25:         if source.provenance.get(LEGAL_CLEARED_KEY) is not True:
  26:             raise ValueError(
  27:                 f"copyrighted span: source {source.source_id!r} not legally "
  28:                 f"cleared for audit bundle redistribution"
  29:             )

 succeeded in 890ms:
   1: """Manifest builder — collects all bundle content + computes SHA256s.
   2: 
   3: Per `.codex/slices/slice_004/architecture_proposal.md` §"manifest_builder".
   4: 
   5: Given a ScopeDecision + EvidencePool + VerifiedReport (the research chain
   6: output), serializes each to canonical JSON, snapshots cited source
   7: texts, and assembles a BundleManifest with SHA256 anchors over every
   8: content file.
   9: 
  10: The manifest itself is YAML-serialized (canonical sort keys); GPG signs
  11: that YAML. External verifiers re-serialize the manifest and re-verify
  12: the signature.
  13: """
  14: 
  15: from __future__ import annotations
  16: 
  17: import hashlib
  18: import json
  19: from datetime import datetime, timezone
  20: from typing import Any
  21: 
  22: import yaml
  23: 
  24: from polaris_graph.audit_bundle.bundle_schema import (
  25:     BUNDLE_VERSION,
  26:     BundleManifest,
  27:     FileEntry,
  28: )
  29: from pathlib import Path
  30: 
  31: from polaris_graph.audit_bundle.snapshot_sources import (
  32:     snapshot_sources,
  33:     snapshot_sources_with_reachable,
  34: )
  35: from polaris_graph.audit_bundle.sovereignty_guard import (
  36:     assert_all_pool_sources_legal_cleared,
  37: )
  38: from polaris_graph.generator2.provenance import extract_tokens
  39: from polaris_graph.generator2.verified_report import VerifiedReport
  40: from polaris_graph.retrieval2.evidence_pool import EvidencePool
  41: from polaris_graph.scope.scope_decision import ScopeDecision
  42: 
  43: 
  44: POLARIS_VERSION = "6.2.0"
  45: 
  46: # Canonical filenames inside the bundle. External verifiers depend on
  47: # these; do not rename without bumping bundle_version.
  48: FILE_SCOPE_DECISION = "scope_decision.json"
  49: FILE_EVIDENCE_POOL = "evidence_pool.json"
  50: FILE_VERIFIED_REPORT = "verified_report.json"
  51: FILE_METADATA = "metadata.json"
  52: FILE_REVIEWER_README = "REVIEWER_README.md"
  53: SOURCES_DIR = "sources"
  54: 
  55: 
  56: def _sha256_bytes(data: bytes) -> str:
  57:     return hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
  58: 
  59: 
  60: def _serialize_json_canonical(model: Any) -> bytes:
  61:     """Pydantic model -> deterministic UTF-8 JSON bytes.
  62: 
  63:     sort_keys + no extra whitespace beyond standard separators ensures
  64:     re-serialization on the verifier side produces identical bytes →
  65:     SHA256 stable across implementations.
  66:     """
  67:     if hasattr(model, "model_dump"):
  68:         payload = model.model_dump(mode="json")
  69:     else:
  70:         payload = model
  71:     return json.dumps(
  72:         payload,
  73:         sort_keys=True,
  74:         ensure_ascii=False,
  75:         separators=(",", ":"),
  76:     ).encode("utf-8")
  77: 
  78: 
  79: def _assert_cited_spans_reachable(report: VerifiedReport, snapshots: dict) -> None:
  80:     """Raise ValueError if any cited span exceeds its snapshot's reachable_chars.
  81: 
  82:     Validates the UNION of tokens parsed from `sentence.sentence_text` and
  83:     `sentence.provenance_tokens`, deduped by `token.raw`. Missing source_id =>
  84:     unreachable. Span end > reachable_chars => unreachable.
  85:     """
  86:     seen: set[str] = set()
  87:     for section in report.sections:
  88:         if section.section_status == "dropped":
  89:             continue
  90:         for sentence in section.verified_sentences:
  91:             if not sentence.verifier_pass:
  92:                 continue
  93:             tokens = list(extract_tokens(sentence.sentence_text))
  94:             for raw in sentence.provenance_tokens:
  95:                 tokens.extend(extract_tokens(raw))
  96:             for tok in tokens:
  97:                 if tok.raw in seen:
  98:                     continue
  99:                 seen.add(tok.raw)
 100:                 entry = snapshots.get(tok.source_id)
 101:                 if entry is None:
 102:                     raise ValueError(
 103:                         f"cited span unreachable after snapshot: source "
 104:                         f"{tok.source_id!r} not in snapshots (token={tok.raw!r})"
 105:                     )
 106:                 if not (0 <= tok.span_start < tok.span_end <= entry.reachable_chars):
 107:                     raise ValueError(
 108:                         f"cited span unreachable after snapshot: source "
 109:                         f"{tok.source_id!r} token={tok.raw!r} span="
 110:                         f"{tok.span_start}-{tok.span_end} reachable={entry.reachable_chars}"
 111:                     )
 112: 
 113: 
 114: def _safe_source_filename(source_id: str) -> str:
 115:     """Build a tar-safe filename from a source_id.
 116: 
 117:     source_id is opaque (uuid by default, but could be 'src-1' etc). We
 118:     sanitize aggressively: only [a-zA-Z0-9_-]. Anything else becomes _.
 119:     Length-capped at 100 chars to avoid pathological inputs.
 120:     """
 121:     safe = "".join(
 122:         c if c.isalnum() or c in "-_" else "_" for c in source_id
 123:     )[:100]
 124:     return f"{SOURCES_DIR}/{safe}.txt"
 125: 
 126: 
 127: def build_manifest_and_files(
 128:     decision: ScopeDecision,
 129:     pool: EvidencePool,
 130:     report: VerifiedReport,
 131: ) -> tuple[BundleManifest, dict[str, bytes]]:
 132:     """Build the bundle manifest + content-file dict.
 133: 
 134:     Returns:
 135:         (manifest, files) where files is {path: content_bytes}. The
 136:         caller (bundle_builder) packs files into a tarball and writes
 137:         the manifest.yaml + manifest.yaml.asc alongside.
 138: 
 139:     Raises:
 140:         ValueError if report.pipeline_verdict != 'success'.
 141:     """
 142:     if report.pipeline_verdict != "success":
 143:         raise ValueError(
 144:             f"cannot build audit bundle for verdict "
 145:             f"{report.pipeline_verdict!r}; report must be successful"
 146:         )
 147: 
 148:     files_bytes: dict[str, bytes] = {}
 149: 
 150:     # 1. Slice 001 ScopeDecision
 151:     sd_bytes = _serialize_json_canonical(decision)
 152:     files_bytes[FILE_SCOPE_DECISION] = sd_bytes
 153: 
 154:     # 2. Slice 002 EvidencePool
 155:     ep_bytes = _serialize_json_canonical(pool)
 156:     files_bytes[FILE_EVIDENCE_POOL] = ep_bytes
 157: 
 158:     # 3. Slice 003 VerifiedReport
 159:     vr_bytes = _serialize_json_canonical(report)
 160:     files_bytes[FILE_VERIFIED_REPORT] = vr_bytes
 161: 
 162:     # 4. Sovereignty: refuse to ship verbatim spans for non-cleared sources
 163:     assert_all_pool_sources_legal_cleared(pool)
 164: 
 165:     # 4b. Per-source snapshots + cited-span reachability guard
 166:     snapshot_entries = snapshot_sources_with_reachable(report, pool)
 167:     _assert_cited_spans_reachable(report, snapshot_entries)
 168:     for source_id, entry in snapshot_entries.items():
 169:         files_bytes[_safe_source_filename(source_id)] = entry.text.encode("utf-8")
 170:     snapshots = {sid: e.text for sid, e in snapshot_entries.items()}
 171: 
 172:     # 4b. REVIEWER_README ships verbatim in every bundle as metadata content_type.
 173:     readme_path = Path(__file__).parent / "REVIEWER_README.md"
 174:     files_bytes[FILE_REVIEWER_README] = readme_path.read_bytes()
 175: 
 176:     # 5. Bundle metadata (versions + creation timestamp)
 177:     metadata = {
 178:         "bundle_version": BUNDLE_VERSION,
 179:         "polaris_version": POLARIS_VERSION,
 180:         "created_at_utc": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat().replace(
 181:             "+00:00", "Z"
 182:         ),
 183:         "generator_model": report.generator_model,
 184:         "decision_id": decision.decision_id,
 185:         "pool_id": report.pool_id,
 186:         "report_id": report.report_id,
 187:         "source_snapshot_count": len(snapshots),
 188:     }
 189:     files_bytes[FILE_METADATA] = json.dumps(
 190:         metadata,
 191:         sort_keys=True,
 192:         ensure_ascii=False,
 193:         separators=(",", ":"),
 194:     ).encode("utf-8")
 195: 
 196:     # Build FileEntry list with content_type tags
 197:     file_entries: list[FileEntry] = []
 198:     content_type_by_path = {
 199:         FILE_SCOPE_DECISION: "scope_decision",
 200:         FILE_EVIDENCE_POOL: "evidence_pool",
 201:         FILE_VERIFIED_REPORT: "verified_report",
 202:         FILE_METADATA: "metadata",
 203:     }
 204:     for path, content in files_bytes.items():
 205:         ct = content_type_by_path.get(
 206:             path,
 207:             "source_snapshot" if path.startswith(SOURCES_DIR + "/") else "metadata",
 208:         )
 209:         file_entries.append(
 210:             FileEntry(
 211:                 path=path,
 212:                 sha256=_sha256_bytes(content),
 213:                 size_bytes=len(content),
 214:                 content_type=ct,  # type: ignore[arg-type]
 215:             )
 216:         )
 217: 
 218:     # Sort entries deterministically for stable manifest output
 219:     file_entries.sort(key=lambda f: f.path)
 220: 
 221:     manifest = BundleManifest(
 222:         decision_id=decision.decision_id,
 223:         pool_id=report.pool_id,
 224:         report_id=report.report_id,
 225:         generator_model=report.generator_model,
 226:         polaris_version=POLARIS_VERSION,
 227:         files=file_entries,
 228:     )
 229:     return manifest, files_bytes
 230:

Pharmaceuticals (Basel).2025. ## Abstract **Background:** Obesity and type 2 diabetes (T2D) are major public health concerns. Tirzepatide has shown promise in recent clinical trials. This systematic review and meta-analysis aim to evaluate the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in adults with obesity or type 2 diabetes, compared to placebo, GLP-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs), and insulin. **Method**: PubMed, Embase, and the Cochrane Library were searched on 17 January 2024, focusing on phase II and III randomized controlled trials (RCTs). We included studies involving adults with T2D or obesity, comparing tirzepatide to placebo, GLP-1 RAs, or insulin. The primary outcomes were the proportion of participants achieving \u22655%, \u226510%, and \u226515% weight loss targets. Secondary outcomes included changes in body weight, waist circumference, HbA1c levels, and blood pressure. Safety outcomes focused on adverse event rates. Meta-analyses were performed, and risk of bias was assessed using the Cochrane Risk-of-Bias tool version 2. **Results**: Fourteen RCTs involving 14,713 patients were \n\n[...]\n\nist tirzepatide: a systematic review and meta-analysis.](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35579691/) Karagiannis T, Avgerinos I, Liakos A, Del Prato S, Matthews DR, Tsapas A, Bekiari E.Karagiannis T, et al.Diabetologia. 2022 Aug;65(8):1251-1261. doi: 10.1007/s00125-022-05715-4. Epub 2025 Sep 9. ### Authors [Haixia Guan](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Guan+H&cauthor_id=40926359)[1](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40926359/#short-view-affiliation-1 \"Department of Endocrinology, Guangdong Provincial People's\n\n[...]\n\nli Lilly and Company, Shanghai, China. * 5 Department of Endocrinology, Peking University People's Hospital, Beijing, People's Republic of China. * PMID: **40926359** * PMCID: [PMC12515763](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12515763/) * DOI: [10.1111/dom.70047](https://doi.org/10.1111/dom.70047) Item in Clipboard Full text links Cite Display options Display options Format ## Abstract **Aim:** To evaluate the long-term efficacy and safety data at 104 weeks in tirzepatide-treated participants with type 2 diabetes who had inadequate\n\n[...]\n\no receive tirzepatide (5, 10, or 15 mg) or insulin glargine. The primary efficacy endpoint was change in HbA1c levels from baseline to 104 weeks. Key secondary endpoints were changes in body weight and the proportion of participants achieving HbA1c <7.0%. Safety endpoints included the incidence of treatment-emergent adverse events (AEs) and hypoglycaemia. **Results:** This post-hoc analysis included 1,500 participants. At Week 104, participants in the tirzepatide groups had significantly greater mean reduction in HbA1c (5 mg: -2.3%, 10 mg: -2.5%, 15 mg: -2.6%) compared with the insulin glargine group (-1.0%) (p < 0.001). Participants in the tirzepatide groups had significantly greater reduction in body weight (5 mg: -7.6 kg, 10 mg: -10.0 kg, 15 mg: -11.4 kg) compared with the insulin glargine group (2.1 kg) (p < 0.001). Significantly more participants in the tirzepatide group achieved HbA1c <7.0% compared with the insulin glargine group (p < 0.001). The incidence of hypoglycaemia was lower in the tirzepatide groups, and gastrointestinal AEs were mild or moderate in severity. **Conclusions:** Tirzepatide significantly improved glycaemic control and body weight reduction compared to insulin gla\n\n[...]\n\n study population for change in HbA1c. Tirzepatide versus Semaglutide Once Weekly in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes Published June 25, 2021 N Engl J Med 2021;385:503-515 DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2107519 Abstract Background Tirzepatide is a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist that is under development for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. The efficacy and safety of once-weekly tirzepatide as compared with semaglutide, a selective GLP-1 receptor agonist, are unknown. Methods In an open-label, 40-week, phase 3 trial, we randomly assigned 1879 patients, in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, to receive tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg or semaglutide at a dose of 1 mg. At baseline, the mean glycated hemoglobin level was 8.28%, the mean age 56.6 years, and the mean weight 93.7 kg. The primary end point was the change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. Results The estimated mean change from baseline in the glycated hemoglobin level was \u22122.01 percentage points, \u22122.24 percentage points, and \u22122.30 percentage points with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of tirzepatide, respectively, and \u22121.86 percentage points with semaglutide; the estimated differences between the 5-mg, 10-mg, and 15-\n\n[...]\n\nMethods In an open-label, 40-week, phase 3 trial, we randomly assigned 1879 patients, in a 1:1:1:1 ratio, to receive tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg or semaglutide at a dose of 1 mg. At baseline, the mean glycated hemoglobin level was 8.28%, the mean age 56.6 years, and the mean weight 93.7 kg. The primary end point was the change in the glycated hemoglobin level from baseline to 40 weeks. Results The estimated mean change from baseline in the glycated hemoglobin level was \u22122.01 percentage points, \u22122.24 percentage points, and \u22122.30 percentage points with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of tirzepatide, respectively, and \u22121.86 percentage points with semaglutide; the estimated differences between the 5-mg, 10-mg, and 15-mg tirzepatide groups and the semaglutide group were \u22120.15 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], \u22120.28 to \u22120.03; P=0.02), \u22120.39 percentage points (95% CI, \u22120.51 to \u22120.26; P 8.5% [\u226469 or >69 mmol per mol]). Tirzepatide was initiated at a dose of 2.5 mg once weekly, and the doses were increased by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks until the randomly assigned dose was reached. The final dose was then maintained for the duration of the trial. Semaglutide was initiated at a dose of 0.25 mg once weekly, and the dose was doubled every 4 weeks until 1 mg was reached. The final dose was then maintained for the duration of the trial.20 Dose de-escalation was not allowed. The initiation of new antihyperglycemic medications was allowed ac\n\n[...]\n\neme, described in the Statistical Analyses Methods section in the Supplementary Appendix, Figs. One immediate event was reported in the tirzepatide 15-mg group. \u00b6 Diabetic retinopathy was confirmed by funduscopic examination. Glycemic End Points At 40 weeks, reductions in the mean glycated hemoglobin level with tirzepatide at a dose of 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg were \u22122.01 percentage points, \u22122.24 percentage points, and \u22122.30 percentage points, respectively, as compared with \u22121.86 percentage points with semaglutide (Figure 1A). All tirzepatide doses were found to be superior to semaglutide, with estimated treatment differences of \u22120.15 percentage points (95% confidence interval [CI], \u22120.28 to \u22120.03; P=0.02) with tirzepatide 5 mg, \u22120.39 percentage points (95% CI, \u22120.51 to \u22120.26; P<0.001) with tirzepatide 10 mg, and \u22120.45 percentage points (95% CI, \u22120.57 to \u22120.32; P<0.001) with tirzepatide 15 mg. The glycated hemoglobin level over time is shown in (Figure 1B). A total of 82 to 86% of the patients who received tirzepatide and 79% of those who received semaglutide had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to less than 7.0%. A total of 69 to 80% of the patients who received tirzepatide and 64% of those who received semaglutide had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to 6.5% or less, and 27 to 46% and 19%, respectively, had a decrease in the glycated hemoglobin level to less than 5.7% (Fig. 1C). A subgroup analysis involving patients with a glycated hemoglobin level above 8.5% showed mean reductions in the glycated hemoglobin level of \u22123.22 percentage points with tirzepatide at a dose of 15 mg as compared with \u22122.68 percentage points with semaglutide (Fig. S8). Figure 1 With the use of the efficacy estimand at 40 weeks, greater reductions in the fasting serum glucose level were observed with all tirzepatide doses than with semaglutide (Figure 1D and Fig. S9). Diabetes Obes Metab.2025 Aug. ## Abstract **Aims:** Tirzepatide reduced weight significantly more than placebo in adults with obesity/overweight who had already achieved \u22655% weight reduction with a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention (randomized population) in SURMOUNT-3, a phase 3, 72-week, randomized, double-blind clinical trial. This analysis evaluated health-related quality of life (HRQoL) with tirzepatide versus placebo treatment in the SURMOUNT-3 randomized population and selected subgroups. **Materials and methods:** The randomized population received placebo (N = 292) or tirzepatide maximum tolerated dose (N = 287) for 72 weeks. Bhat , BSc Student [1]* [1] Department of Cellular and Molecular Biology, Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA 02138 *Corresponding Author: prishabhat@college.harvard.edu Abstract Introduction: Type 2 diabetes is a chronic metabolic disorder driven by insulin resistance and impaired \u03b2 -cell function. Glucagon -like peptide -1 receptor agonists (GLP -1 RAs) have become central to diabetes management by improving insulin secretion, reducing glucagon, promoting weight loss, and lowering cardiovascular risk. Dual GLP -1/glucose -dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) receptor agonists, such as tirzepatide, represent an emerging therapeutic class designed to enhance metabolic outcomes by activating complementary incretin pathways. This review evaluates how dual GLP -1/GIP agonists differ from GLP -1 RAs in mechanism, efficacy, safety, and therapeutic potential. Methods: A systematic review was conducted using PubMed, Scopus, and th\n\n[...]\n\n. Primary outcomes were hemoglobin A1c reduction and weight change; secondary outcomes included safety, cardiovascular measures, and \u03b2 -cell function. Results: Across phase 3 trials, dual agonists achieved greater reductions in hemoglobin A1c (up to 2.4%) and body weight (up to 22%) compared to GLP -1 RAs. These effects reflect synergistic actions on insulin secretion, glucagon suppression, appetite regulation, and adipocyte metabolism. Dual agonists also demonstrated higher rates of normoglycemia \n\n[...]\n\n RESEARCH IN NATURAL AND CLINICAL SCIENCE AND TECHNOLOGY (URNCST) JOURNAL Read more URNCST Journal articles and submit your own today at: https://www.urncst.com Bhat | URNCST Journal (2026): Volume 10 , Issue 4 Page 2 of 8 DOI Link : https://doi.org/10.26685/urncst.1029 Figure 1 . Glycemic Control GLP -1 RAs reduce HbA1c by approximately 1.0 \u2013 1.5% in major trials, including SUSTAIN -6 (semaglutide) and REWIND (dulaglutide) [ 1, 9\u201312 ]. A greater proportion of participants receiving tirzepatide achieved HbA1c levels below 5.7% across baseline HbA1c categories [2] . Long -term extension studies have reported sustained glycemic control with tirzepatide compared with GLP -1 receptor agonists [14] .   75:     "direct_quote": "Abstract Purpose: A systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted to combine the data available from clinical trials and evaluate the clinical efficacy and safety of tirzepatide in people with type 2 diabetes (T2D). Methods: We systematically searched the MEDLINE, Embase, Cochrane Library, and clinical trials registries (https://clinicaltrials.gov) up to 25 March 2022 for randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that compared tirzepatide with placebo or active hypoglycemic drugs in subjects with T2D. Heterogeneity was judged by the I2 value and Cochran\u2019s Q test. The randomized effects model was adopted to calculate risk ratios and weighted mean differences (WMDs). The primary outcome was the change from baseline in HbA1c levels. Secondary efficacy endpoints were fasting serum glucose (FSG), change of body weight, blood pressure, fasting lipid profiles, and safety indexes. Results: Six trials comprising 6,579 subjects (4,410 in the tirzepatide group and 2,054 in the control group) fulfilled the pre-specified criteria and were included in the study. Tirzepatide treatment resulted in reducing HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% confidence intervals [CIs]: \u22121.44, \u22120.56), FSG (WMD, \u221221.50 mg/dl; 95% CI: \u221234.44, \u22128.56), body weight (WMD: \u22127.99 kg; 95% CI \u221211.36, \u22124.62), and blood pressure and ameliorated fasting lipid profiles, without increasing hypoglycemia, either as monotherapy or an add-on therapy. Tirzepatide increased the risk of gastrointestinal adverse events mainly in add-on therapy b\n\n[...]\n\nsafety indexes. Results: Six trials comprising 6,579 subjects (4,410 in the tirzepatide group and 2,054 in the control group) fulfilled the pre-specified criteria and were included in the study. Tirzepatide treatment resulted in reducing HbA1c (WMD: -1.07%; 95% confidence intervals [CIs]: \u22121.44, \u22120.56), FSG (WMD, \u221221.50 mg/dl; 95% CI: \u221234.44, \u22128.56), body weight (WMD: \u22127.99 kg; 95% CI \u221211.36, \u22124.62), and blood pressure and ameliorated fasting lipid profiles, without increasing hypoglycemia, either as monotherapy or an add-on therapy. Tirzepatide increased the risk of gastrointestinal adverse events mainly in add-on therapy but not in terms of \n\n[...]\n\non-communicable chronic diseases, resulting in disabilities, expensive complications, and even shortening of life expectancy (Saeedi et al., 2019). Unfortunately, in the world, the prevalence rate of diabetes in the population of age 20\u201379 years was 10.5% in 2021, which was about 536 million 600 thousand; by 2045, it is expected to increase up to 12.2%, which will be about 783 million 200 thousand (Sun H. et al., 2022). According to the pathophysiology of diabetes, different antihyperglycemic drugs have been developed for clinical application. Since human insulin was approved in 1982, the FDA has\n\n[...]\n\ndose\u2013response analysis. Finally, the results of SURPASS-5 were published in 2022 and were not included. We performed and updated a systematic review and meta-analysis to evaluate the safety and efficacy of tirzepatide in patients with T2D. 2 Methods 2.1 Protocol Our systematic review and meta-analysis was executed and reported in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) statement (Page et al., 2021). The protocol was registered in PROSPERO (No. CRD42022319442). 2.2 Search strategy The Embase, MEDLINE, and Cochrane databases and clinical trials registries (https://clinicaltrials.gov) were comprehensively searched prior to 25 March 2022, without limitations on language, race, or country. The following terms were\n\n[...]\n\nretin OR dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist) AND (type 2 diabetes OR diabetes) AND (randomized controlled trial). The search strategy was adjusted to meet the requirements of each database. 2.3 Inclusion and exclusion criteria We included randomized controlled trials (RCTs), which lasted at least 12 weeks, that assessed and compared the efficacy and safety of tirzepatide with other hypoglycemic agents or placebos in T2D patients (\u226518 years\n\n[...]\n\nd), diagnosed according to the World Health Organization (1999) or American Diabetes Association (1997) criteria. Reviews, letters, case reports, nonhuman studies, editorials, commentaries, expert opinions, non-RCTs, and meta-analyses were excluded. 2.4 Outcome measures of efficacy and safety The change in HbA1c from the baseline was considered the primary outcome of efficacy. The secondary endpoints included proportions of patients with HbA1c 5% of usual body weight (American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association Task Force on Practice Guidelines, 2014). Our meta-analysis results demonstrated that loss of body weight in the tirzepatide group was from 7.25 kg to 10.36 kg, whether used as monotherapy (7.40 kg) or add-on therapy (8.11 kg). Tirzepatide therapy led to a greater target of the percentage of patients who reached the body weight reduction of \u22655%, \u226510%, and \u226515%, whether used as monotherapy or add-on therapy. More interestingly, weight loss was observed at all doses of \n\n[...]\n\nRAs and exhibited that, compared to placebo, GLP-1 RAs led to significant body weight reduction (Htike et al., 2017). As for semaglutide administered in patients with T2D, compared with placebo, subcutaneous semaglutide led to body weight loss (WMD: -2.73 kg and -4.09 kg, for 0.5 mg and 1 mg, respectively). Oral administration showed similar effects (Zhong et al., 2021). For treatment with dulaglutide in patients with T2D, the mean weight loss was 0.73 kg in the 0.75 mg dulaglutide group and 1.27 kg in the 1.5 mg dulaglutide group (Qie et al., 2020). Our meta-analysis included semaglutide 1 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg as the control and presented that tirzepatide reduced body weight greater than the two GLP-1 RAs and resulted in a higher proportion of body weight reaching \u22655%, \u226510%, and \u226515%. Blood pressure and lipid profiles are the risk factors of cardiovasc\n\n[...]\n\npatide showed improvements in blood glucose control and, at the same time, did not increase the risk of mild-to-moderate and severe hypoglycemia, both when used as monotherapy and add-on therapy. The rate of blood glucose <70 mg/dl and <54 mg/dl was 34.77% and 7.64% in SURPASS-4 (Del Prato et al., 2021) and 61.13% and 16.34% in SURPASS-5 (Dahl et al., 2022), respectively, which appeared to be higher. The use of sulfonylurea as a part of the background treatment in SURPASS-4 and the use of insulin glargine in SURPASS-5 were probably the reason. When hypoglycemia was considered, GLP-1 RA was referred to be semaglutide 1 mg in our meta-analysis. Rates in patients with hypoglycemia who received semaglutide 0.5 mg and 1 mg were 23.1% and 21.7%, respectively, which was similar to those of the placebo (21.2%) in the SUSTAIN-6 study (Marso et al., 2016). There were no differences in hypoglycemia between tirzepatide and semaglutide 1 mg. In a word, the risk of hypoglycemia seems to be low for subcutaneous tirzepatide; however, it increases when used in c\n\n[...]\n\necond, the trial duration was from 26 to 52 weeks, which was not enough to evaluate the hard endpoints, such as cardiovascular events and all-cause death. Third, although there was no publication bias, the p-value of Begg\u2019s test and Egger\u2019s test was 0.06 and 0.091, respectively, and the funnel plot did not seem to be symmetric, which suggested that we should pay attention to the possible publication bias. Conclusion In conclusion, our systematic review and meta-analysis provides comprehensive estimates of the",

  84:     "direct_quote": "## EDITORIAL ## From needles to pills: oral GLP-1 therapy enters the obesity arena Gaetano Santulli 1,2,3* ## Abstract Obesity remains a major global health challenge, driving type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and other complications. Despite effective lifestyle and surgical interventions, pharmacotherapy uptake has historically been limited. Injectable GLP-1 receptor agonists, including semaglutide and tirzepatide, have redefined expectations for medical obesity therapy, achieving 15-20% weight reductions in clinical trials. However, barriers such as injections, cost, and adherence limit their real-world use. In September 2025, two pivotal phase-3 trials of oral GLP-1 therapies were published. ATTAIN-1 evaluated orforglipron in 3,127 adults with obesity over 72 weeks, demonstrating a mean weight loss of 11.2%, \u2265 10% weight loss in 54.6%, and improvements in cardiometabolic parameters. OASIS-4 studied oral semaglutide 25 mg in 307 adults over 64 weeks, showing a mean weight loss of 13.6%, \u2265 10% weight loss in 63%, and favorable metabolic changes. Both agents exhibited gastrointestinal adverse events consistent with the GLP-1 class; additionally, orforglipron had five mild pancreatitis cases, while oral semaglutide reported mild dysesthesia. These results confirm that oral GLP-1 receptor agonists can produce clinically meaningful weight loss and metabolic benefits, expanding options beyond injectables. Real-world adoption will hinge on adherence, tolerability, long-term\n\n[...]\n\nP-1 therapies [3-5]. The question now is not whether oral GLP-1s work, but how they will fit into the rapidly evolving therapeutic landscape for obesity. \u00a9 The Author(s) 2025. Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, which permits use, sharing, adaptation, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, an\n\n[...]\n\nesent the closest we have come to that reality. ## The trials at a glance ## Orforglipron - The ATTAIN-1 trial - Participants: 3,127 adults with obesity, without diabetes. - Duration: 72 weeks. - Mean BMI: ~37 kg/m\u00b2. - Results: Mean weight loss of - 11.2% at the 36 mg dose; 54.6% achieved \u2265 10% weight loss; -Improvements in waist circumference, blood pressure, and lipids. - Safety: Gastrointestinal adverse events frequent but manageable; Five cases of mild pancreatitis; Modest increase in heart rate observed. This was a lar\n\n[...]\n\n and provides a solid foundation for regulatory consideration [1]. ## Oral semaglutide 25 mg - The OASIS-4 trial - Participants: 307 adults with obesity, without diabetes. - Duration: 64 weeks. - Mean BMI: ~37 kg/m\u00b2. - Results: Mean weight loss of - 13.6%; 63% achieved \u2265 10% weight loss; Improvements in HbA1c, triglycerides, and inflammatory markers. - Safety: Gastrointestinal side effects consistent with GLP-1 class; reports of mild dysesthesia in a small number of participants. OASIS-4 was smaller\n\n[...]\n\nntions - ATTAIN-1: participants received healthy lifestyle advice. - OASIS-4: participants were prescribed a standardized 500 kcal daily deficit.  120:     "direct_quote": "Abstract\nThe effects of tirzepatide, a glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist, on weight reduction after successful intensive lifestyle intervention are unknown. This double-blind, placebo-controlled trial randomized (1:1) adults with body mass index \u226530 or \u226527 kg/m2 and at least one obesity-related complication (excluding diabetes), who achieved \u22655.0% weight reduction after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention, to tirzepatide maximum tolerated dose (10 or 15 mg) or placebo once weekly for 72 weeks (n = 579). The treatment regimen estimand assessed effects regardless of treatment adherence in the intention-to-treat population. The coprimary endpoint of additional mean per cent weight change from randomization to week 72 was met with changes of \u221218.4% (standard error (s.e.) 0.7) with tirzepatide and 2.5% (s.e. 1.0) with placebo (estimated treatment difference \u221220.8 percentage points (95% confidence interval (CI) \u221223.2%, \u221218.5%; P < 0.001). The coprimary endpoint of the percentage of participants achieving additional weight reduction \u22655% was met with 87.5% (s.e. 2.2) with tirzepatide and 16.5% (s.e. 3.0) with placebo achieving this threshold (odds ratio 34.6%; 95% CI 19.2%, 62.6%; P < 0.001). The most common adverse events with tirzepatide were gastrointestinal, with most being mild to moderate in severity. Tirzepatide provided substantial additional reduction in body weight in participants who had achieved \u22655.0% weight r\n\n[...]\n\nfter successful intensive lifestyle intervention are unknown. This double-blind, placebo-controlled trial randomized (1:1) adults with body mass index \u226530 or \u226527 kg/m2 and at least one obesity-related complication (excluding diabetes), who achieved \u22655.0% weight reduction after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention, to tirzepatide maximum tolerated dose (10 or 15 mg) or placebo once weekly for 72 weeks (n = 579). The treatment regimen estimand assessed effects regardless of treatment adherence in the intention-to-treat population. The coprimary endpoint of additional mean per cent weight change from randomization to week 72 was met with changes of \u221218.4% (standard error (s.e.) 0.7) with tirzepatide and 2.5% (s.e. 1.0) with placebo (estimated treatment difference \u221220.8 percentage points (95% confidence interval (CI) \u221223.2%, \u221218.5%; P < 0.001). The coprimary endpoint of the percentage of participants achieving additional weight reduction \u22655% was met with 87.5% (s.e. 2.2) with tirzepatide and 16.5% (s.e. 3.0) with placebo achieving this threshold (odds ratio 34.6%; 95% CI 19.2%, 62.6%; P < 0.001). The most common adverse events with tirzepatide were gastrointestinal, with most being mild to moderate in severity. Tirzepatide provided substantial additional reduction in body weight in participants who had achieved \u22655.0% weight reduction with intensive lifestyle intervention. Every spring residents of the city\u2019s Strathearn neighbourhood planted root vegetables in long rows and grew tomatoes out of old tires while rain barrels collected water. Every fall the community gathered to share in the bounty. Last year the garden was uprooted. The lot the garden stood on was sold for almost $600,000. Immediately after the sale local developer Platinum Living tore up the earth and began construction on an eight-unit row house. These built-to-rent homes are set to hit the market this summer and if Platinum Living\u2019s other builds are any indication, they are going to cost well over market averages. Built at the whim of the landowner, without public debate or deliberation at city council, this development breezed through the approval process thanks to an extended campaign by law makers at all levels of government to deregulate municipal zoning. For the last 18 months the federal government has been incentivizing city councils across the country to restructure their zoning bylaws. Enrollment growth accounted for less than 15% of this increase (range: 7.2% (CT)\u201413.3% (x-ray)), while the impact of payment increases was far greater and varied widely (range: 7.6% (nuclear)\u201454.0% (x-ray)). The share of DI spend- ing growth attributable to improvements in access to care was: x-ray (6.5%); ultrasound (19.1%); CT (30.4%); MR (49.0%); and nuclear (30.5%). The contribution of volume growth to overall spending growth ranged from 10.5% for MR to 24.1% for CT. Service intensity growth accounted for less than 10% of spending growth for x-ray, CT and MR; 17.9% and 33.0% of spending growth for ultrasound and nuclear were due to service intensity growth, respectively. CONCLUSION: Improved access to care explains approximately 30%\u201350% of the growth in Medicare spending for advanced diagnostic imaging services. state\polaris_restart\codex_verdict_cleanup_iter_5.txt:1693:365f334:outputs/audits/continuous/40b4d30_audit.md:59:**P3.1 — Audit-trail integrity model (F-15 + F-16 set the precedent).** F-15 committed a working-tree edit on a previously-committed audit file (`bb60495_audit.md`, P1=2→P1=1 + shortened pre-flight). F-16 backfilled a `BACKFILL`-prefixed brief for `3bac322`. Both are honest in this case: 40b4d30's commit message explicitly states "F-15: commit the working-tree edit on bb60495_audit.md that has been dirty across cycles 4+5" and the backfill brief begins with `**BACKFILL**`. Threat-model question: would a future commit silently roll an audit edit into a multi-file batch? Today's commits prove the discipline works because the messages are explicit. The git history retains both versions (97b9c1f's original + 40b4d30's revised). Recommendation: **document the audit-trail integrity model** in `.codex/AUDIT_CYCLE_PROTOCOL_v2.md` — "post-commit edits to audit files require an explicit fix ID (F-N) and a commit message line identifying the modification; silent-batch edits violate the model." Future cycle-9+ should grep commit messages for audit-file edits and verify each carries an F-ID. Tag: **guardrail** — codify the rule before someone abuses it.

state\polaris_restart\codex_verdict_cleanup_iter_5.txt:2444:outputs/audits/continuous/40b4d30_audit.md:59:**P3.1 — Audit-trail integrity model (F-15 + F-16 set the precedent).** F-15 committed a working-tree edit on a previously-committed audit file (`bb60495_audit.md`, P1=2→P1=1 + shortened pre-flight). F-16 backfilled a `BACKFILL`-prefixed brief for `3bac322`. Both are honest in this case: 40b4d30's commit message explicitly states "F-15: commit the working-tree edit on bb60495_audit.md that has been dirty across cycles 4+5" and the backfill brief begins with `**BACKFILL**`. Threat-model question: would a future commit silently roll an audit edit into a multi-file batch? Today's commits prove the discipline works because the messages are explicit. The git history retains both versions (97b9c1f's original + 40b4d30's revised). Recommendation: **document the audit-trail integrity model** in `.codex/AUDIT_CYCLE_PROTOCOL_v2.md` — "post-commit edits to audit files require an explicit fix ID (F-N) and a commit message line identifying the modification; silent-batch edits violate the model." Future cycle-9+ should grep commit messages for audit-file edits and verify each carries an F-ID. Tag: **guardrail** — codify the rule before someone abuses it.

state\polaris_restart\codex_verdict_cleanup_iter_4.txt:1125:365f334:outputs/audits/continuous/40b4d30_audit.md:59:**P3.1 — Audit-trail integrity model (F-15 + F-16 set the precedent).** F-15 committed a working-tree edit on a previously-committed audit file (`bb60495_audit.md`, P1=2→P1=1 + shortened pre-flight). The git history retains both versions (97b9c1f's original + 40b4d30's revised). Recommendation: **document the audit-trail integrity model** in `.codex/AUDIT_CYCLE_PROTOCOL_v2.md` — "post-commit edits to audit files require an explicit fix ID (F-N) and a commit message line identifying the modification; silent-batch edits violate the model." Future cycle-9+ should grep commit messages for audit-file edits and verify each carries an F-ID. Tag: **guardrail** — codify the rule before someone abuses it.

state\polaris_restart\codex_verdict_cleanup_iter_4.txt:1901:.\outputs\audits\continuous\40b4d30_audit.md:59:**P3.1 — Audit-trail integrity model (F-15 + F-16 set the precedent).** F-15 committed a working-tree edit on a previously-committed audit file (`bb60495_audit.md`, P1=2→P1=1 + shortened pre-flight). F-16 backfilled a `BACKFILL`-prefixed brief for `3bac322`. Both are honest in this case: 40b4d30's commit message explicitly states "F-15: commit the working-tree edit on bb60495_audit.md that has been dirty across cycles 4+5" and the backfill brief begins with `**BACKFILL**`. Threat-model question: would a future commit silently roll an audit edit into a multi-file batch? Today's commits prove the discipline works because the messages are explicit. The git history retains both versions (97b9c1f's original + 40b4d30's revised). Recommendation: **document the audit-trail integrity model** in `.codex/AUDIT_CYCLE_PROTOCOL_v2.md` — "post-commit edits to audit files require an explicit fix ID (F-N) and a commit message line identifying the modification; silent-batch edits violate the model." Future cycle-9+ should grep commit messages for audit-file edits and verify each carries an F-ID. Tag: **guardrail** — codify the rule before someone abuses it.

.codex\I-arch-001d\codex_brief_verdict_iter_3.txt:11895:outputs\full_scale_v30_phase2_run14\clinical\clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm\corpus_approval.json:480:  "rejected_source_urls": [],
.codex\I-arch-001d\codex_brief_verdict_iter_3.txt:11973:outputs\codex_runs\5_1_prep_briefing_pack\iter_2\codex_stdout.log:1634:.\outputs\codex_findings\dr_output_pass_1\codex_stderr.log:2987:outputs\polaris_graph\GEMINI_E2E_20260316_092353.json:44586:  "draft_report": "# Comparative Analysis of Adhesion Testing Methods for Polymer Coatings: Methodologies, Performance, and Regulatory Compliance\n\n## Abstract\n\nThis report evaluates adhesion testing methodologies for polymer coatings, specifically comparing pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials. The scope encompasses methodological standards, failure analysis, and environmental impacts on bond integrity, drawing from established ASTM and ISO protocols. Exclusions involve non-polymer substrates, dynamic fatigue testing not covered by static adhesion standards, and proprietary industrial methods lacking public documentation. Evidence limitations include variability in industry-specific documentation and potential gaps in recent silicone-specific comparative data regarding long-term exposure. The analysis synthesizes regulatory requirements with practical performance metrics to guide method selection. Findings highlight critical distinctions in failure mode classification and the influence of surface preparation on test validity. Regulatory compliance and warranty documentation requirements are also addressed to ensure operational reliability. This structured comparison aims to reduce testing ambiguity and improve consistency in coating performance evaluation across diverse service conditions. Furthermore, economic implications of testing choices are considered to balance accuracy with operational costs. The report concludes with strategic recommendations for optimizing test selection based on material properties. Overall, the study provides a comprehensive framework for stakeholders seeking to validate coating durability and adherence quality in complex engineering applications.\n\n## Standardized Test Method Definitions and ASTM ISO Frameworks\n\nAdhesion testing frameworks for polymer coatings are governed by a complex array of ASTM and ISO standards that define methodological rigor and compliance thresholds [CITE:ev_057ad7d7fadf39c2]. These standardized protocols establish the baseline for comparing pull-off, cross-cut, and peel tests across epoxy, urethane, and silicone materials [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266]. The regulatory landscape distinguishes between quantitative tensile measurements and qualitative visual assessments, each serving specific diagnostic purposes within the coating lifecycle [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. Pull-off adhesion testing applies tensile force perpendicular to the substrate to measure coating resistance to separation [CITE:ev_057ad7d7fadf39c2]. This method is utilized to gage adhesive strength of the coating once applied and cured as per manufacturer's guidelines [CITE:ev_2b7ca35715831266]. The pull-off adhesion tester measures adhesion of coatings to metal, wood, concrete, and rigid substrates, providing versatility across material classes [CITE:ev_0a2f7e8a1aa2c852]. Pull-off test data provides quantitative indication of bond tensile strength [CITE:ev_448284e198da9b99]. Industrial-grade adhesives typically have tensile strength ranging from 10 MPa to over 60 MPa depending on formulation [CITE:ev_a85f361a2bdff124]. Hydroxyapatite coatings showed repeatable adhesion strength of 3.4 \u00b1 1.0 MPa across both ASTM test methods [CITE:ev_5bd33c7a02d9cd28]. ASTM C633-01 test uses 25 mm diameter cylindrical blocks with 38 mm length for adhesion testing [CITE:ev_e9f86ec169988ea]. ASTM D4541 and BS EN ISO 4624 describe pull-off adhesion testing standards for protective coatings [CITE:ev_befc6654b3c8f5ad]. The pull-off test is standardized by ISO 4624:2023 and is widely used to quantify adhesion [CITE:ev_7267f03f92de9267]. ASTM D7234 defines the standard test method for pull-off adhesion strength on concrete using portable testers [CITE:ev_3a7d481c74614432]. Pull-off testing follows ISO 4624, ASTM D4541, and D7234 standards for evaluating coating peel strength [CITE:ev_0ffc84130522e5ce]. Valid pull-off test requires coating to cover at least 50% of stamp surface after detachment [CITE:ev_d0994f38fa60bebe].\n\nThe Cross-Cut Test provides qualitative evaluation while the Pull-Off Method provides quantitative evaluation of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d]. This distinction is critical when selecting methods for epoxy, urethane, or silicone systems, as quantitative data may be required for structural warranty validation. Cross-Cut Test classifies adhesion results into six levels according to the degree of peeling after tape removal [CITE:ev_cd6ffc422440dd18]. The procedure involves making lattice-pattern cuts through the coating to the substrate, applying adhesive tape, and peeling in one motion [CITE:ev_4add28fe26ddd5e3]. ASTM D3359 outlines standardized test methods for evaluating adhesion of coatings to metallic substrates using pressure-sensitive tape [CITE:ev_815bc543dbac8f04]. This standard is widely recognized for evaluating coating adhesion on metal substrates using the tape test method [CITE:ev_59cf9ed236eb49bd]. ISO 2409 specifies the cross-cut test method for evaluating adhesion of coatings on sheet metal parts [CITE:ev_067cc0abd42529d2]. ASTM D3359 defines adhesion as molecular attraction and mechanical bonds between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_a930d76424157b92]. ASTM D3359 procedures assess the adhesion of coating films specifically on metallic substrates [CITE:ev_949116df55b626a8]. The tape test involves applying and removing pressure-sensitive tape over cuts made in the film [CITE:ev_b737d3f89fc71af0]. The tape test is qualitative and cannot precisely assess quantitatively the actual strength of the adhesion [CITE:ev_389d441cc3fe3ba4]. Cross-Cut Test uses pressure-sensitive tape spread over cuts and rapidly pulled off [CITE:ev_25929b033d8bd227]. Adhesion is rated by inspecting the cut area for coating removal relative to classifications 0B through 5B [CITE:ev_d23f5ac9162865a3]. Cross-cut adhesion test evaluation ranges from 0B representing complete detachment to 5B representing no detachment [CITE:ev_d0d69c4b450fa1e3]. ASTM D3359 Standard Test Method for Measuring Adhesion by Tape Test was codified in 1974 [CITE:ev_cee97c994fefc9b1]. ASTM D3359 is a standard test method for measuring adhesion by tape test [CITE:ev_23c66a42fadbecc5]. Crosshatch adhesion testing follows ASTM D3359 standard with grid cuts through coating [CITE:ev_2365cdb310fc8adf]. ASTM D3359 is the standard method for rating adhesion by tape test [CITE:ev_d2c3f9dace34682e].\n\nPeel testing represents a third category, measuring the force required to strip a flexible coating from a substrate [CITE:ev_6e9c26b6c7722bbe]. Peel tests measure adhesive strength by pulling one material away from another at a specific angle and speed [CITE:ev_f0f1594bc5d9dd20]. This method is suitable for designs where bonded edges experience prying or bending [CITE:ev_1be768df70c46a07]. Peel and adhesion strength testing measures bond strength when force attempts separation [CITE:ev_27f9ac91a5f22e9b]. Comprehensive adhesive suitability requires comparing peel adhesion, tensile strength, and shear resistance [CITE:ev_ac671c6a322fe8bd]. The peel test is a method used in materials science to measure the bond strength between two materials [CITE:ev_6bcf28f6b901e894]. Peel strength is usually reported in units of force per width such as N/mm or lb/in [CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a]. Peel testing methods include T-peel and 90-degree peel tests standardized under ASTM D1876 and D6862 [CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca]. Standard testing procedures evaluate peel force under constant temperature 23\u00b11\u00b0C and 50\u00b15% RH [CITE:ev_4420310a01914e20]. Peel test values represent an average between 5 and 35mm of peel for each of two tests [CITE:ev_e84426d0da5ec986]. T-peel testing was performed according to BS 5350-C12:1994/ISO 11339:1993 standard on aluminium alloy [CITE:ev_f93ca08a9dc0966f]. Chinese national standard GB/T 2790-1995 specifies the 180\u00b0 peel strength test method steps and requirements [CITE:ev_4ec63e8773cfcd3e]. ISO 8510-2 covers 180-degree peel test for flexible-bonded-to-rigid test specimen assembly [CITE:ev_75d316de9669a24a]. The T shaped peel test ASTM D1876 is most widely used for thin-gauge metal adherends [CITE:ev_e3a5ffd4420e583a].\n\nTable 1 summarizes the comparative frameworks for these primary adhesion testing methods, highlighting standard designations and operational limits.\n\n| Test Method | Primary Standard (ASTM/ISO) | Substrate Suitability | Measurement Type | Thickness Limit |\n| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |\n| Pull-Off | ASTM D4541, ISO 4624 | Metal, Concrete, Rigid | Quantitative (MPa) | N/A [CITE:ev_0ffc84130522e5ce] |\n| Cross-Cut | ASTM D3359, ISO 2409 | Metal, Plastic, Rigid | Qualitative (0B-5B) | 250 \u00b5m [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207] |\n| Peel | ASTM D1876, ISO 11339 | Flexible-to-Rigid | Quantitative (N/mm) | N/A [CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca] |\n| Knife/Scrape | ASTM D6677 | Hard Coatings | Qualitative/Force | N/A [CITE:ev_3f498ca240e7a532] |\n\nThickness constraints significantly influence method selection for polymer coatings. The Cross-Cut Test is limited by coating thickness with a maximum of 250 micrometers [CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207]. Cross-cut test is applicable for layer thicknesses up to about 250 \u00b5m [CITE:ev_df003953c7f7a2d0]. This limitation renders the cross-cut method unsuitable for thick urethane or epoxy linings often exceeding this threshold. Conversely, the test method covers determination of practical adhesion strength of hard ceramic coatings on metal and ceramic substrates [CITE:ev_f6c7a8ecd3f37045]. Test method is commonly used for coating thicknesses in the range of 0.1 to 30 micrometers [CITE:ev_8858c4a9dba538b2]. Adhesion pull-off strength testing determines the overall quality and integrity of an installed coating system [CITE:ev_c357968c8f3b130a]. Cross hatch tape testing is intended for laboratory use on coatings under 5 mils thickness [CITE:ev_c8b75c8d91e7faea]. Scrape test adhesion is measured in kilograms of force required for removal [CITE:ev_73f6c6e661c92e30]. ASTM D6677 is the standard method for evaluating adhesion by knife [CITE:ev_3f498ca240e7a532]. Cross-cut test is especially suitable for thin layers on rigid substrates like metal or plastic [CITE:ev_bb2ddf4927283db1]. The crosscut adhesion test is a standard ASTM or ISO procedure for dry coatings [CITE:ev_49557422c8fc215a].\n\nConflicting evidence exists regarding the fundamental capabilities of these tests and specific procedural dimensions. One source states the pull-off adhesion test measures the strength of the bond between a coating and the surface [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00]. However, conflicting evidence indicates the test method does not measure fundamental adhesion strength of the bond between coating and substrate [CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d]. This discrepancy suggests a distinction between practical field adhesion and theoretical interfacial strength. Regarding cut patterns, evidence states cross-cut test uses a tool to cut a right-angle lattice pattern through the coating to the substrate [CITE:ev_183b421be46e5e8b]. Conversely, other evidence indicates X-cut tape test uses a sharp tool to make an X-shaped cut through the coating to substrate [CITE:ev_377599bde46462b1]. ASTM D3359 accommodates both via Method A (X-Cut) and Method B (Cross-Cut Tape Test), with Method B preferred for laboratory or controlled environments [CITE:ev_a673f1e5d40af5bb]. Test Method A (X-Cut Tape Test) is designed primarily for field use with X-shaped cut in coating [CITE:ev_e46b804dd9b8226]. Cross-Hatch Test is typically used on witness samples of the same substrate material [CITE:ev_26a79c112c1e2bd7].\n\nProcedural variations also appear in peel test specimen dimensions. Evidence indicates DOWSIL ALLGUARD coating adhesion test uses cheesecloth strip 1 inch by 12 inches embedded in wet coating [CITE:ev_234e921351755d24]. In conflict, another source states peel test uses 1 inch wide by 6 inch long fabric strip embedded in coating base layer [CITE:ev_14c32495c2f3b455]. These variations likely reflect specific manufacturer protocols versus general standardized methods. ISO 4624 is referenced as a global standard for pull-off adhesion testing compliance [CITE:ev_a9eaff2972823a42]. Traditional adhesion tests like ISO/JIS cross-hatch and ASTM tape tests only evaluate final adhesion strength without explaining adhesion mechanisms [CITE:ev_60e99e00ac021116]. Surface free energy and adhesion parameter measurements are proposed as complementary methods to traditional cross-cut tape tests [CITE:ev_d77bb9a950ab7132]. Adhesion testing mechanics are complicated when polymer adhesive and metal substrate have different elastic moduli [CITE:ev_2fc7d6f56363052a]. This complexity is particularly relevant for silicone and urethane coatings which exhibit different elastic properties compared to epoxy systems. Peel and pull-off data should not be compared since testing mechanisms differ [CITE:ev_aabe5f967b113f45]. Four common methods exist for testing adhesion of protective coatings and linings per ASTM standards [CITE:ev_c53e1e15e6c509b0]. Common adhesion measuring techniques are performed using a knife or a pull-off adhesion tester [CITE:ev_ac710f7b1e8e52c6].\n\nSpecific material considerations emerge within the evidence regarding epoxy and silicone systems. Results showed good agreement between hot water-adhesion tests and TxDOT peel tests [CITE:ev_6c07ce38730c09f8]. This suggests environmental conditioning impacts epoxy coating performance assessment. The majority of bond systems underwent tensile adhesion strength testing using a button-to-plate configuration [CITE:ev_e552dd2b19ec130e]. Silicone contamination impacts bond performance, necessitating rigorous testing protocols [CITE:ev_e552dd2b19ec130e]. Video How to Perform an Adhesion Test Patch provides more information on roof coating adhesion tests [CITE:ev_537eee4252bc6670]. This indicates field verification is critical for urethane and silicone roof applications. Adhesion test must be conducted prior to estimating coating restoration project per Inland adhesion testing procedures [CITE:ev_61c41c390dad6a70]. Adhesive performance evaluation after testing includes checking bonding strength, color stability, surface integrity, and chemical stability [CITE:ev_eecd425930fc2a46]. Tensile or shear tests measure strength between bonded materials for adhesives [CITE:ev_2248a1dd659c4419]. The study objective was to identify an optimal adhesive for the pull-off test providing higher adhesion [CITE:ev_f8a2b17c15a40670]. Pull-off method provides values for peel stress allowing quick determination and comparison of coating adhesion [CITE:ev_03d5cf9642fafd22]. The proposed adhesion testing method uses direct pull-off strength measurements at various corrosion stages [CITE:ev_6592a406ace27776]. Field adhesion test recommended when laboratory test cannot be performed on-site [CITE:ev_f569492a9905b1d9]. Field adhesion test also called hand pull test verifies proper sealant installation [CITE:ev_7658ca3c2e89c2e9]. There is no universally accepted test for conformal coating adhesion [CITE:ev_c30e40e01bb9b77c]. SAE J400 standard outlines specialized coating adhesion test method using Instron testing machine [CITE:ev_b26b994900339f4e]. Latter two ASTM methods evaluate tensile or pull-off strength of coating [CITE:ev_d7edcf2ffc730737]. The test method was originally developed for ductile coatings applied to metal substrates [CITE:ev_5b8a012a381ea628]. Site adhesion testing must be verified according to criteria on page 35 to confirm proper surface preparation [CITE:ev_97840fd9469c02ca]. Nitto Denko employs adhesive pull strength evaluation using JIS Z0237 and ASTM standards [CITE:ev_d063340009e4394c]. Mechanical analysis services include testing for adhesive strength, tensile strength, yield stress [CITE:ev_1f32724ed272e28]. All structural applications must be tested by the Dow test lab for surface preparation recommendations [CITE:ev_24d4477c668a302]. A similar testing program is needed for concrete coatings to enable laboratory verification [CITE:ev_49d5639552dc7c2]. Adhesive testing products include epoxy glue, polyurethane glue, and organic silicone among many others [CITE:ev_5b179a7386ac92ce]. Tensile bulk tests were performed using long dogbone specimens to characterize adhesives at room temperature [CITE:ev_59bf06dce7ffc5c5]. Higher yield strength values obtained in compression test than in tensile test for all adhesives [CITE:ev_73023757450301de]. Study of size dependence of bulk specimen was carried out to evaluate scale factors in adhesive testing [CITE:ev_26d2fc42825fb10]. NPL and partners developed a tape peel test applicable to PCB or component surfaces [CITE:ev_7416ad3c2e89c2e9]. ATS offers ASTM paint adhesion testing to determine adhesive performance according to industry standards [CITE:ev_22e6e69ce64f2b]. Peel tests involve pulling flexible material from a surface at defined angles [CITE:ev_f06a21a7fab15411].\n\n**Key Findings**\n\n*   Pull-off methods (ASTM D4541, ISO 4624) provide quantitative tensile strength data suitable for thick epoxy and urethane systems, whereas cross-cut tests (ASTM D3359) offer qualitative ratings limited to coatings under 250 \u00b5m [CITE:ev_84a08742318ed15d][CITE:ev_a256fbf9c6062207].\n*   Conflicting evidence exists regarding whether pull-off tests measure fundamental adhesion strength or practical bond integrity, indicating a need for method selection based on specific performance criteria [CITE:ev_d62d99d84a009e00][CITE:ev_1a095db8b38db71d].\n*   Peel testing standards (ASTM D1876, ISO 11339) quantify force per width (N/mm) for flexible-to-rigid bonds, essential for silicone and urethane membrane evaluations [CITE:ev_16c2d5e21aa178ca][CITE:ev_44fea46094d83d2a].\n*   ASTM D3359 encompasses both X-cut and lattice patterns, with the lattice method preferred for laboratory precision and the X-cut for field applications [CITE:ev_183b421be46e5e8b][CITE:ev_e46b804dd9b8226].\n*   Environmental conditions and substrate elastic moduli significantly influence test outcomes, requiring controlled temperature and humidity during peel and tensile assessments [CITE:ev_2fc7d6f56363052a][CITE:ev_4420310a01914e20].\n\n![ASTM Standard Timeline](data:image/png;base64,

  11: ## P1-002-novel — model_copy bypasses validator + evaluator_agrees consistency
  12: 
  13: **Resolution**: rebuild VerifiedSentence via the full constructor (which runs validators), AND explicitly set `evaluator_agrees=False`:
  14: 
  15: ```python
  16: def _redact_sentence(sent: VerifiedSentence) -> VerifiedSentence:
  17:     """Mark a sentence as failed during sovereignty cascade.
  18: 
  19:     Uses VerifiedSentence(**fields) constructor (runs validators) rather
  20:     than model_copy(update=...) which bypasses them.
  21:     """
  22:     fields = sent.model_dump()
  23:     fields["verifier_pass"] = False
  24:     fields["drop_reason"] = "invalid_token"
  25:     fields["evaluator_agrees"] = False  # P1-002 — must mirror verifier_pass
  26:     # Per VerifiedSentence validator: verifier_pass=False with evaluator_agrees=True
  27:     # is forbidden. Setting both False keeps the model valid.
  28:     return VerifiedSentence(**fields)
  29: ```
  30: 
  31: The construct-via-`__init__` path runs all `@field_validator`s and `@model_validator`s, surfacing any invariant violations as `ValidationError` instead of allowing silent contradiction.
  32: 
  33: Same pattern for section_status update:
  34: 
  35: ```python
  36: def _drop_section(section: Section, kept: list[VerifiedSentence]) -> Section:
  37:     fields = section.model_dump()
  38:     fields["verified_sentences"] = [s.model_dump() for s in kept]
  39:     fields["section_verify_pass_rate"] = 0.0
  40:     fields["section_status"] = "dropped"
  41:     return Section(**fields)
  42: ```

.codex\I-arch-001d\codex_diff_audit_iter_1.txt:7806:.codex\I-arch-001d\codex_brief_verdict_iter_1.txt:4730:outputs\I-gen-002_q5_phase3_verify\policy\carney_pharmacare_bill_c64_evidence\evidence_pool.json:120:    "direct_quote": "Poster Presentations POSTER SESSION I HEALTH CARE USE & POLICY STUDIES\u2014 Adherence/Compliance PHP1 EFFECT OF PRESCRIPTION COPAY ON MEDICATION UTILIZATION Gause D, Doyle JJ, Plauschinat C Novartis Pharmaceuticals, East Hanover, NJ, USA OBJECTIVE: To assess impact of change in prescription copay on change in medication utilization among patients taking three common brand name medications: atorvastatin for dyslipide- mia, pioglitazone for diabetes, or valsartan for hypertension. METHODS: The Medstat MarketScan database was used to identify patients taking a study drug (atorvastatin, pioglitazone, or valsartan) in both 2003 and 2004. Patients had to have continuous pharmacy coverage, at least one \ufb01ll in each year, and be 30 day supply the impact of copay on days supply was less but still statistically signi\ufb01cant. CONCLUSION: Policy and bene\ufb01t decision makers need to consider the impact of patient copay on persistence for chronic diseases such as diabetes, hyper- lipidemia, and hypertension. PHP2 ADHERENCE TO EVIDENCE-BASED GUIDELINES AND MEDICATION COMPLIANCE FOR MULTIPLE CHRONIC DISEASES IN A MANAGED CARE DATABASE Burch SP, Priest JL, Cook CL, Cantrell CR GlaxoSmithKline, Research Triangle Park, NC, USA OBJECTIVE: To examine both adherence to treatment guide- lines and medication compliance for commercially insured patients with common chronic conditions including asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), congestive heart failure (CHF), coronary artery disease (CAD), \n\n[...]\n\nre excluded. A sub-analysis of articles was conducted to determine the rationale for selection of the cut-point. RESULTS: The review identi\ufb01ed 98 studies with 103 measures of adherence. These studies investi- gated adherence to cardiovascular drugs (39.8%), hormones and synthetic substitutes (33.0%) and others (28.2%). The following types of measures were used: medication possession ratio (MPR) (45.6%), \ufb01xed gap between re\ufb01lls (38.8%), proportion of days covered (7.8%) and others (7.8%). Dichotomous measures were used by 32.0% of the studies. A cut point was speci\ufb01ed for 35.9% measures of adherence. Ninety percent (6646) of the 7396 bene\ufb01ciaries with MS enrolled in a Part D plan\u2014about 50% higher than the rate of enrollment in the general Medicare popu- lation. Female gender (OR = 1.5, 1.3\u20131.6), MRDD bene\ufb01t use (OR = 2.6, 2.4\u20132.8), higher HCC score (OR = 1.07, 1.03\u20131.10), other drug coverage during the MRDD (OR = 1.6, 1.5\u20131.7), were associated with Part D enrollment. There were regional differences as well. Older age (OR = 0.9, 0.9\u20130.9) and death within 6 months (OR = 0.3, 0.3\u20130.4) were associated with not enrolling in Part D. Separate regressions for the RA and MS populations produced similar results. CONCLUSION: With the inception of Medicare Part D, most MRDD bene\ufb01ciaries with RA and MS enrolled in Part D plans. RESULTS: Aggregate DI service spending grew at an annual rate of 15.2% during 2002\u20132005, and varied substantially by modal- ity (x-ray 10.2%, ultrasound 11.7%, CT 19.6%, MR 18.5%, nuclear 15.0%). Enrollment growth accounted for less than 15% of this increase (range: 7.2% (CT)\u201413.3% (x-ray)), while the impact of payment increases was far greater and varied widely (range: 7.6% (nuclear)\u201454.0% (x-ray)). The share of DI spend- ing growth attributable to improvements in access to care was: x-ray (6.5%); ultrasound (19.1%); CT (30.4%); MR (49.0%); and nuclear (30.5%). The contribution of volume growth to overall spending growth ranged from 10.5% for MR to 24.1% for CT. Service intensity growth accounted for less than 10% of spending growth for x-ray, CT and MR; 17.9% and 33.0% of spending growth for ultrasound and nuclear were due to service intensity growth, respectively. CONCLUSION: Improved access to care explains approximately 30%\u201350% of the growth in Medicare spending for advanced diagnostic imaging services.
